lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Be thou assured, good Cassio, I will do

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: All my abilities in thy behalf.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Good madam, do: I warrant it grieves my husband,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: As if the case were his.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, that’s an honest fellow. Do not doubt, Cassio,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But I will have my lord and you again

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: As friendly as you were.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Bounteous madam,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Whatever shall become of Michael Cassio,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: He’s never any thing but your true servant.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I know’t; I thank you. You do love my lord:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: You have known him long; and be you well assured

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: He shall in strangeness stand no further off

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Than in a polite distance.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Ay, but, lady,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That policy may either last so long,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Or feed upon such nice and waterish diet,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Or breed itself so out of circumstance,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That, I being absent and my place supplied,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: My general will forget my love and service.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Do not doubt that; before Emilia here

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I give thee warrant of thy place: assure thee,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If I do vow a friendship, I’ll perform it

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To the last article: my lord shall never rest;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I’ll watch him tame and talk him out of patience;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: His bed shall seem a school, his board a shrift;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I’ll intermingle every thing he does

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: With Cassio’s suit: therefore be merry, Cassio;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: For thy solicitor shall rather die

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Than give thy cause away.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Madam, here comes my lord.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Madam, I’ll take my leave.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, stay, and hear me speak.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Madam, not now: I am very ill at ease,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Unfit for mine own purposes.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Well, do your discretion.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Ha! I like not that.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What dost thou say?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nothing, my lord: or if—I know not what.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Was not that Cassio parted from my wife?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cassio, my lord! No, sure, I cannot think it,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That he would steal away so guilty-like,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Seeing you coming.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I do believe ’twas he.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How now, my lord!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I have been talking with a suitor here,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: A man that languishes in your displeasure.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Who is’t you mean?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, your lieutenant, Cassio. Good my lord,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If I have any grace or power to move you,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: His present reconciliation take;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: For if he be not one that truly loves you,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: That errs in ignorance and not in cunning,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I have no judgment in an honest face:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I prithee, call him back.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Went he hence now?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Ay, sooth; so humbled

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: That he hath left part of his grief with me,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To suffer with him. Good love, call him back.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not now, sweet Desdemona; some other time.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But shall’t be shortly?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The sooner, sweet, for you.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Shall’t be to-night at supper?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: No, not to-night.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To-morrow dinner, then?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I shall not dine at home; I meet the captains at the citadel.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, then, to-morrow night; or Tuesday morn;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: On Tuesday noon, or night; on Wednesday morn:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I prithee, name the time, but let it not

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Exceed three days: in faith, he’s penitent;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And yet his trespass, in our common reason—

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Save that, they say, the wars must make examples

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Out of their best—is not almost a fault

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To incur a private cheque. When shall he come?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Tell me, Othello: I wonder in my soul,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What you would ask me, that I should deny,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Or stand so mammering on. What! Michael Cassio,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: That came a-wooing with you, and so many a time,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: When I have spoke of you dispraisingly,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Hath ta’en your part; to have so much to do

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To bring him in! Trust me, I could do much—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Prithee, no more: let him come when he will;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I will deny thee nothing.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, this is not a boon;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: ’Tis as I should entreat you wear your gloves,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Or feed on nourishing dishes, or keep you warm,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Or sue to you to do a peculiar profit

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To your own person: nay, when I have a suit

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Wherein I mean to touch your love indeed,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: It shall be full of poise and difficult weight

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And fearful to be granted.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I will deny thee nothing:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Whereon, I do beseech thee, grant me this,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To leave me but a little to myself.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Shall I deny you? no: farewell, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell, my Desdemona: I’ll come to thee straight.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Emilia, come. Be as your fancies teach you;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Whate’er you be, I am obedient.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Excellent wretch! Perdition catch my soul,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But I do love thee! and when I love thee not,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Chaos is come again.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My noble lord—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What dost thou say, Iago?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Did Michael Cassio, when you woo’d my lady,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Know of your love?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He did, from first to last: why dost thou ask?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But for a satisfaction of my thought;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: No further harm.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why of thy thought, Iago?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I did not think he had been acquainted with her.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, yes; and went between us very oft.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Indeed!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Indeed! ay, indeed: discern’st thou aught in that?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is he not honest?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Honest, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Honest! ay, honest.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My lord, for aught I know.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What dost thou think?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Think, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Think, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: By heaven, he echoes me,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As if there were some monster in his thought

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Too hideous to be shown. Thou dost mean something:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I heard thee say even now, thou likedst not that,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: When Cassio left my wife: what didst not like?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And when I told thee he was of my counsel

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In my whole course of wooing, thou criedst “Indeed!”

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And didst contract and purse thy brow together,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As if thou then hadst shut up in thy brain

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Some horrible conceit: if thou dost love me,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Show me thy thought.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My lord, you know I love you.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I think thou dost;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And, for I know thou’rt full of love and honesty,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And weigh’st thy words before thou givest them breath,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Therefore these stops of thine fright me the more:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For such things in a false disloyal knave

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Are tricks of custom, but in a man that’s just

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: They are close delations, working from the heart

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That passion cannot rule.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For Michael Cassio,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I dare be sworn I think that he is honest.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I think so too.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Men should be what they seem;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Or those that be not, would they might seem none!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Certain, men should be what they seem.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, then, I think Cassio’s an honest man.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nay, yet there’s more in this:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I prithee, speak to me as to thy thinkings,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As thou dost ruminate, and give thy worst of thoughts

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The worst of words.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Good my lord, pardon me:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though I am bound to every act of duty,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am not bound to that all slaves are free to.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Utter my thoughts? Why, say they are vile and false;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As where’s that palace whereinto foul things

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Sometimes intrude not? who has a breast so pure,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But some uncleanly apprehensions

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Keep leets and law-days and in session sit

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With meditations lawful?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thou dost conspire against thy friend, Iago,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If thou but think’st him wrong’d and makest his ear

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A stranger to thy thoughts.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I do beseech you—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though I perchance am vicious in my guess,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As, I confess, it is my nature’s plague

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To spy into abuses, and oft my jealousy

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Shapes faults that are not—that your wisdom yet,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: From one that so imperfectly conceits,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Would take no notice, nor build yourself a trouble

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Out of his scattering and unsure observance.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It were not for your quiet nor your good,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nor for my manhood, honesty, or wisdom,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To let you know my thoughts.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What dost thou mean?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Good name in man and woman, dear my lord,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is the immediate jewel of their souls:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who steals my purse steals trash; ’tis something, nothing;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: ’Twas mine, ’tis his, and has been slave to thousands:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But he that filches from me my good name

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Robs me of that which not enriches him

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And makes me poor indeed.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: By heaven, I’ll know thy thoughts.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You cannot, if my heart were in your hand;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nor shall not, whilst ’tis in my custody.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, beware, my lord, of jealousy;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It is the green-eyed monster which doth mock

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The meat it feeds on; that cuckold lives in bliss

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who, certain of his fate, loves not his wronger;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But, O, what damned minutes tells he o’er

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Who dotes, yet doubts, suspects, yet strongly loves!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O misery!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Poor and content is rich and rich enough,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But riches fineless is as poor as winter

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To him that ever fears he shall be poor.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Good heaven, the souls of all my tribe defend

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: From jealousy!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why, why is this?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Think’st thou I’ld make a lie of jealousy,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To follow still the changes of the moon

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: With fresh suspicions? No; to be once in doubt

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is once to be resolved: exchange me for a goat,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: When I shall turn the business of my soul

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To such exsufflicate and blown surmises,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Matching thy inference. ’Tis not to make me jealous

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To say my wife is fair, feeds well, loves company,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is free of speech, sings, plays and dances well;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Where virtue is, these are more virtuous:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nor from mine own weak merits will I draw

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The smallest fear or doubt of her revolt;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For she had eyes, and chose me. No, Iago;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ll see before I doubt; when I doubt, prove;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And on the proof, there is no more but this—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Away at once with love or jealousy!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am glad of it; for now I shall have reason

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To show the love and duty that I bear you

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With franker spirit: therefore, as I am bound,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Receive it from me. I speak not yet of proof.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Look to your wife; observe her well with Cassio;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Wear your eye thus, not jealous nor secure:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I would not have your free and noble nature,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Out of self-bounty, be abused; look to’t:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I know our country disposition well;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In Venice they do let heaven see the pranks

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: They dare not show their husbands; their best conscience

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is not to leave’t undone, but keep’t unknown.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Dost thou say so?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She did deceive her father, marrying you;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And when she seem’d to shake and fear your looks,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She loved them most.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And so she did.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, go to then;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She that, so young, could give out such a seeming,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To seal her father’s eyes up close as oak—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He thought ’twas witchcraft—but I am much to blame;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I humbly do beseech you of your pardon

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For too much loving you.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I am bound to thee for ever.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I see this hath a little dash’d your spirits.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not a jot, not a jot.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ faith, I fear it has.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I hope you will consider what is spoke

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Comes from my love. But I do see you’re moved:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am to pray you not to strain my speech

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To grosser issues nor to larger reach

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Than to suspicion.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I will not.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Should you do so, my lord,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My speech should fall into such vile success

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As my thoughts aim not at. Cassio’s my worthy friend—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My lord, I see you’re moved.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: No, not much moved:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I do not think but Desdemona’s honest.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Long live she so! and long live you to think so!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And yet, how nature erring from itself—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Ay, there’s the point: as—to be bold with you—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Not to affect many proposed matches

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Of her own clime, complexion, and degree,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Whereto we see in all things nature tends—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Foh! one may smell in such a will most rank,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Foul disproportion thoughts unnatural.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But pardon me; I do not in position

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Distinctly speak of her; though I may fear

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Her will, recoiling to her better judgment,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: May fall to match you with her country forms

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And happily repent.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell, farewell:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If more thou dost perceive, let me know more;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Set on thy wife to observe: leave me, Iago:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Going. My lord, I take my leave.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why did I marry? This honest creature doubtless

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Sees and knows more, much more, than he unfolds.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Returning. My lord, I would I might entreat your honour

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To scan this thing no further; leave it to time:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Though it be fit that Cassio have his place,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For sure, he fills it up with great ability,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet, if you please to hold him off awhile,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You shall by that perceive him and his means:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Note, if your lady strain his entertainment

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With any strong or vehement importunity;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Much will be seen in that. In the mean time,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Let me be thought too busy in my fears—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As worthy cause I have to fear I am—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And hold her free, I do beseech your honour.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Fear not my government.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I once more take my leave.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: This fellow’s of exceeding honesty,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And knows all qualities, with a learned spirit,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of human dealings. If I do prove her haggard,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Though that her jesses were my dear heartstrings,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ld whistle her off and let her down the wind,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To pray at fortune. Haply, for I am black

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And have not those soft parts of conversation

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That chamberers have, or for I am declined

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Into the vale of years—yet that’s not much—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She’s gone. I am abused; and my relief

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Must be to loathe her. O curse of marriage,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That we can call these delicate creatures ours,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And not their appetites! I had rather be a toad,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And live upon the vapour of a dungeon,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Than keep a corner in the thing I love

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For others’ uses. Yet, ’tis the plague of great ones;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Prerogatived are they less than the base;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis destiny unshunnable, like death:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Even then this forked plague is fated to us

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: When we do quicken. Desdemona comes:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How now, my dear Othello!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Your dinner, and the generous islanders

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: By you invited, do attend your presence.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I am to blame.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why do you speak so faintly?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Are you not well?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have a pain upon my forehead here.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: ’Faith, that’s with watching; ’twill away again:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Let me but bind it hard, within this hour

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: It will be well.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Your napkin is too little: He puts the handkerchief from him; and it drops.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let it alone. Come, I’ll go in with you.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I am very sorry that you are not well.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I am glad I have found this napkin:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: This was her first remembrance from the Moor:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My wayward husband hath a hundred times

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Woo’d me to steal it; but she so loves the token,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: For he conjured her she should ever keep it,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That she reserves it evermore about her

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: To kiss and talk to. I’ll have the work ta’en out,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And give’t Iago: what he will do with it

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Heaven knows, not I;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I nothing but to please his fantasy.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How now! what do you here alone?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Do not you chide; I have a thing for you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: A thing for me? it is a common thing—

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To have a foolish wife.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, is that all? What will you give me now

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: For the same handkerchief?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What handkerchief?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: What handkerchief?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Why, that the Moor first gave to Desdemona;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That which so often you did bid me steal.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Hast stol’n it from her?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: No, ’faith; she let it drop by negligence.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And, to the advantage, I, being here, took’t up.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Look, here it is.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: A good wench; give it me.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: What will you do with ’t, that you have been so earnest

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: To have me filch it?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Snatching it. Why, what’s that to you?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: If it be not for some purpose of import,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Give’t me again: poor lady, she’ll run mad

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: When she shall lack it.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Be not acknown on ’t; I have use for it.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Go, leave me.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I will in Cassio’s lodging lose this napkin,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And let him find it. Trifles light as air

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Are to the jealous confirmations strong

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As proofs of holy writ: this may do something.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The Moor already changes with my poison:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Dangerous conceits are, in their natures, poisons.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Which at the first are scarce found to distaste,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But with a little act upon the blood.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Burn like the mines of Sulphur. I did say so:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Look, where he comes!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ha! ha! false to me?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, how now, general! no more of that.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Avaunt! be gone! thou hast set me on the rack:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I swear ’tis better to be much abused

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Than but to know’t a little.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How now, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What sense had I of her stol’n hours of lust?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I saw’t not, thought it not, it harm’d not me:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I slept the next night well, was free and merry;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I found not Cassio’s kisses on her lips:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He that is robb’d, not wanting what is stol’n,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let him not know’t, and he’s not robb’d at all.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am sorry to hear this.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I had been happy, if the general camp,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Pioners and all, had tasted her sweet body,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: So I had nothing known. O, now, for ever

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell the tranquil mind! farewell content!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell the plumed troop, and the big wars,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That make ambition virtue! O, farewell!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell the neighing steed, and the shrill trump,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The spirit-stirring drum, the ear-piercing fife,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The royal banner, and all quality,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Pride, pomp and circumstance of glorious war!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And, O you mortal engines, whose rude throats

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The immortal Jove’s dead clamours counterfeit,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Farewell! Othello’s occupation’s gone!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is’t possible, my lord?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Villain, be sure thou prove my love a whore,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Be sure of it; give me the ocular proof:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or by the worth of man’s eternal soul,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thou hadst been better have been born a dog

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Than answer my waked wrath!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is’t come to this?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Make me to see’t; or, at the least, so prove it,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That the probation bear no hinge nor loop

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To hang a doubt on; or woe upon thy life!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My noble lord—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If thou dost slander her and torture me,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Never pray more; abandon all remorse;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: On horror’s head horrors accumulate;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Do deeds to make heaven weep, all earth amazed;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For nothing canst thou to damnation add

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Greater than that.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O grace! O heaven forgive me!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Are you a man? have you a soul or sense?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: God be wi’ you; take mine office. O wretched fool.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That livest to make thine honesty a vice!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O monstrous world! Take note, take note, O world,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To be direct and honest is not safe.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I thank you for this profit; and from hence

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll love no friend, sith love breeds such offence.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nay, stay: thou shouldst be honest.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I should be wise, for honesty’s a fool

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And loses that it works for.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: By the world,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I think my wife be honest and think she is not;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I think that thou art just and think thou art not.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ll have some proof. Her name, that was as fresh

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As Dian’s visage, is now begrimed and black

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As mine own face. If there be cords, or knives,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Poison, or fire, or suffocating streams,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ll not endure it. Would I were satisfied!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I see, sir, you are eaten up with passion:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I do repent me that I put it to you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You would be satisfied?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Would! nay, I will.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And may: but, how? how satisfied, my lord?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Would you, the supervisor, grossly gape on—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Behold her topp’d?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Death and damnation! O!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It were a tedious difficulty, I think,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To bring them to that prospect: damn them then,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If ever mortal eyes do see them bolster

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: More than their own! What then? how then?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What shall I say? Where’s satisfaction?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It is impossible you should see this,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Were they as prime as goats, as hot as monkeys,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As salt as wolves in pride, and fools as gross

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As ignorance made drunk. But yet, I say,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If imputation and strong circumstances,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Which lead directly to the door of truth,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will give you satisfaction, you may have’t.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Give me a living reason she’s disloyal.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I do not like the office:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But, sith I am enter’d in this cause so far,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Prick’d to’t by foolish honesty and love,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I will go on. I lay with Cassio lately;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And, being troubled with a raging tooth,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I could not sleep.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: There are a kind of men so loose of soul,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That in their sleeps will mutter their affairs:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: One of this kind is Cassio:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In sleep I heard him say “Sweet Desdemona,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Let us be wary, let us hide our loves;”

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And then, sir, would he gripe and wring my hand,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cry “O sweet creature!” and then kiss me hard,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As if he pluck’d up kisses by the roots

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That grew upon my lips: then laid his leg

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Over my thigh, and sigh’d, and kiss’d; and then

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cried “Cursed fate that gave thee to the Moor!”

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O monstrous! monstrous!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, this was but his dream.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But this denoted a foregone conclusion:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis a shrewd doubt, though it be but a dream.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And this may help to thicken other proofs

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That do demonstrate thinly.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ll tear her all to pieces.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, but be wise: yet we see nothing done;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She may be honest yet. Tell me but this,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Have you not sometimes seen a handkerchief

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Spotted with strawberries in your wife’s hand?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I gave her such a one; ’twas my first gift.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I know not that; but such a handkerchief—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am sure it was your wife’s—did I to-day

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: See Cassio wipe his beard with.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If it be that—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If it be that, or any that was hers,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It speaks against her with the other proofs.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, that the slave had forty thousand lives!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: One is too poor, too weak for my revenge.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Now do I see ’tis true. Look here, Iago;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: All my fond love thus do I blow to heaven.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis gone.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Arise, black vengeance, from thy hollow cell!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yield up, O love, thy crown and hearted throne

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To tyrannous hate! Swell, bosom, with thy fraught,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For ’tis of aspics’ tongues!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Yet be content.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, blood, blood, blood!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Patience, I say; your mind perhaps may change.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Never, Iago: Like to the Pontic sea,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Whose icy current and compulsive course

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ne’er feels retiring ebb, but keeps due on

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To the Propontic and the Hellespont,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Even so my bloody thoughts, with violent pace,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Shall ne’er look back, ne’er ebb to humble love,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Till that a capable and wide revenge

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Swallow them up. Now, by yond marble heaven,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In the due reverence of a sacred vow

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I here engage my words.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do not rise yet.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Witness, you ever-burning lights above,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You elements that clip us round about,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Witness that here Iago doth give up

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The execution of his wit, hands, heart,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To wrong’d Othello’s service! Let him command,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And to obey shall be in me remorse,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What bloody business ever. They rise.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I greet thy love,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not with vain thanks, but with acceptance bounteous,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And will upon the instant put thee to’t:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Within these three days let me hear thee say

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That Cassio’s not alive.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: My friend is dead; ’tis done at your request:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But let her live.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Damn her, lewd minx! O, damn her!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Come, go with me apart; I will withdraw,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To furnish me with some swift means of death

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For the fair devil. Now art thou my lieutenant.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am your own for ever.

stage_directions.text: If she be false, O, then heaven mocks itself! I’ll not believe’t.
Not poppy, nor mandragora, Nor all the drowsy syrups of the world, Shall ever medicine thee to that sweet sleep Which thou owedst yesterday.

glosses.definition: I will give him no peace
finicky, insubstantial nourishment — of political caution
a sworn defender; a devoted friend
a dinner-table that serves as a confessional
Iago's pretended pang of suspicion; the first planted seed of doubt in Othello
slink off as if caught in guilt
Othello's loving oxymoron — darling, delightful creature
may damnation seize me — Othello's vow of love, tragically prophetic
jealousy, pictured as a monster that mocks the flesh it feeds on; the phrase is Shakespeare's most enduring coinage for the emotion
a husband whose wife is unfaithful — a term carrying deep public shame in Elizabethan culture

glosses.phrase: I am bound to thee for ever
glosses.definition: I am in your debt for life

glosses.phrase: She did deceive her father, marrying you
glosses.definition: Iago's poisonous reminder: if she deceived him once, she can deceive again
country, skin, and rank — Iago's racist catalogue of reasons Desdemona should have rejected Othello
Venetian standards of beauty; the native type
the straps on a hawk's legs — Othello imagines releasing her
downwind — the direction a hawk is loosed when cast off forever
frequenters of ladies' chambers; idle gallants
the branching curse of cuckolds' horns
handkerchief — the handkerchief that becomes the play's deadly evidence
mere nothings, weightless — but Iago notes they become iron to the jealous; his key insight into how evidence operates in a poisoned mind
the martial drum that rouses the soul
my identity as soldier is gone — the line registers his self-collapse

glosses.phrase: prime as goats, as hot as monkeys
glosses.definition: lustful as goats, as aroused as monkeys — Iago's animal catalogue of alleged lust
shared a bed with Cassio recently — army custom of officers bunking together
the throne-place of my love within my heart
the unchanging heaven — the eternal witness to his oath
the Black Sea — whose current was thought to flow only outward, never backward; Othello's simile for his unstoppable revenge
the Sea of Marmara, lying between the Black Sea and the Aegean
the strait now called the Dardanelles — the onward path of Othello's imagined current
a revenge that contains all and reaches everywhere
Cassio's office, now promised to Iago — the coveted promotion granted

glosses.phrase: I am your own for ever
glosses.definition: Iago's damning declaration — the pledge of total loyalty in service of his deception

annotations.title: The geometry of insinuation
annotations.body:
Iago's temptation of Othello is conducted almost entirely through withheld information. He does not accuse Cassio; he steals away 'guilty-like' and lets Othello notice. He does not impute adultery; he asks whether Cassio knew of the courtship, ventures a half-question, trails off. Othello supplies every conclusion. The technique is surgical.

Notice the grammar. Iago's sentences end in ellipses, conditionals, and negations: 'I cannot think it, / That he would steal away so guilty-like'; 'Men should be what they seem.' Each utterance is a space into which Othello's imagination is invited to step. Nothing Iago says is technically false; everything he withholds becomes the substance of what Othello believes.

This is why the scene is Shakespeare's most influential anatomy of manipulation. It is not about lies. It is about the architecture of implication and the way authority of voice — 'honest Iago' — transforms pauses into evidence.
sources: William Empson, 'Honest in Othello', in *The Structure of Complex Words* (1951)
James L. Calderwood, *The Properties of Othello* (1989)

annotations.title: The naming of the green-eyed monster
annotations.body:
The phrase 'green-eyed monster' is Shakespeare's, coined here, and has become the language's standard emblem for jealousy. The image is precise: a creature 'which doth mock / The meat it feeds on' — a beast that plays with its prey before consuming it, or (some editors argue) that mocks by lying about what is fed to it. Both readings operate at once.

Green was the color of jealousy in Elizabethan physiology, associated with bile and unsettled humors. Cats' eyes, proverbially predatory, complete the image. The monster is 'fineless' — without limit, an appetite without a stopping point. This is a definition of jealousy as a feeding disorder: the emotion consumes what it pretends to protect.

The coinage is part of a longer semantic field Iago opens in this scene — 'conceit', 'surmise', 'seeming' — the vocabulary of knowledge that cannot be grounded.
sources: OED, s.v. 'green-eyed' (citing this passage as first use)
Paul Alpers, *What Is Pastoral?* (1996) on Shakespeare's inventive lexis

annotations.title: Clime, complexion, and degree
annotations.body:
Iago shifts registers here from suspicion to race. Nature in Desdemona, he insinuates, should have repudiated 'many proposed matches / Of her own clime, complexion, and degree' — country, skin, and class. Rejecting Venetian suitors for Othello is, on this account, a departure from nature itself; if she has strayed once from her kind, she may stray again.

The argument is doubly poisonous. It makes Desdemona's original choice evidence of instability, and it invites Othello to internalize a racial vocabulary about himself. Within thirty lines he will call himself 'black', will question whether the 'soft parts of conversation' are beyond him, will speak of his own age and his racial difference as arguments against his desirability. Iago's racism becomes Othello's self-appraisal. This is the scene's most devastating transformation.
sources: Arthur L. Little Jr., *Shakespeare Jungle Fever* (2000)

annotations.title: Trifles light as air
annotations.body:
The most quoted single line in Iago's rhetoric — 'Trifles light as air / Are to the jealous confirmations strong / As proofs of holy writ' — announces the scene's epistemological argument. Iago does not need evidence of Desdemona's guilt; he needs Othello to be jealous. Once Othello is in that state, the handkerchief (a stage prop worth pennies) will function with the authority of scripture.

The line reverses ordinary weights: air becomes holy writ, the negligible becomes the absolute. Shakespeare is describing the physics of a ruined mind. For the jealous, the unit of evidence has changed. Weight is in the viewer, not the viewed.
sources: Thomas M. Greene, 'The Light in Troy', *Comparative Literature* (1982)

annotations.title: The Pontic sea simile and the point of no return
annotations.body:
Othello's oath binding his revenge to the unreversing current of the Black Sea — flowing 'to the Propontic and the Hellespont', never 'ebb to humble love' — is the play's structural hinge. He kneels; Iago kneels beside him; they speak a vow in the shape of a marriage ceremony ('I am your own for ever'). The irony is exact: Othello's marriage to Desdemona has been displaced by a vow to Iago.

Geographically Shakespeare is precise. The Pontic (Black) Sea empties continuously south through the Bosphorus and Hellespont into the Aegean, a current classical geographers from Polybius onward described as unreturning. Othello, an oceanic man, picks the oceanic figure for the irrevocability he has just entered. After this vow, nothing in the play flows backward.

annotations.title: Othello's occupation's gone
annotations.body:
The aria Shakespeare gives Othello after Iago has set the hook — 'Farewell the tranquil mind... farewell the plumed troops... Othello's occupation's gone' — is not only a soldier's lament. It is a valediction to the self he constructed in the Senate scene. Everything he named in that earlier autobiography — the tented field, the moving accidents, the profession as identity — is now past tense.

Read as dramatic action, the speech is premature. Nothing has actually been proven; he has only just become suspicious. But Othello experiences the suspicion as the end of his life, because his self was an epic self, and epic selves do not survive domestic ambiguity. The speech is Shakespeare showing us the cost of a particular kind of heroic identity — the kind Othello needed to become a Venetian commander, the kind that cannot accommodate the private doubts of ordinary marriage.
sources: F. R. Leavis, 'Diabolic Intellect and the Noble Hero', *Scrutiny* (1937)
Paul A. Kottman, *Tragic Conditions in Shakespeare* (2009)

trials.prompt: Iago says 'Ha! I like not that' when he sees Cassio leave Desdemona. What is he actually doing?
options: Expressing genuine disapproval of Cassio's manners
Planting a small seed of suspicion without directly accusing anyone
Warning Othello about a political conspiracy
Complaining about Cassio's military strategy

trials.prompt: Iago uses the image of a 'green-eyed monster' to describe jealousy. What does the monster do in his description?
options: Weeps uncontrollably and cannot be comforted
Mocks the meat it feeds on — it plays with what it consumes
Sleeps for a year and wakes hungrier
Produces monstrous offspring

trials.prompt: In this scene the audience sees Desdemona's innocent behavior clearly. The characters see it filtered through Iago's suggestions. What is this dramatic device called, and how does Shakespeare use it throughout the scene?
options: Soliloquy — the audience hears inner thoughts not available to other characters
Dramatic irony — the audience knows more than the characters and must watch them act on less information
Dumbshow — pantomime of future events precedes the dialogue
Chorus — a commenting voice frames each episode

trials.prompt: Iago argues that Desdemona's decision to reject suitors 'of her own clime, complexion, and degree' is itself suspicious. What ideological move is he making?
options: He is citing the Venetian marriage law
He is converting a free choice into evidence of unnatural appetite, using racist logic to make Othello doubt his own desirability
He is praising her independence of mind
He is noting that she should have married a fellow soldier

trials.prompt: Iago declares that 'Trifles light as air / Are to the jealous confirmations strong / As proofs of holy writ.' What is the core insight?
options: Jealousy makes sufferers obsessed with reading Scripture
For a jealous mind, even trivial objects become as authoritative as Biblical evidence
Jealousy can be cured by reading religious texts
Holy writings often contain proof of infidelity

trials.prompt: The 'Pontic Sea' simile Othello uses for his revenge describes a current that:
options: Flows back and forth with the tides
Flows only one way and never reverses, matching his unstoppable revenge
Evaporates in hot weather
Freezes in winter and thaws in spring

trials.prompt: At the end of the scene, Othello and Iago kneel together and make mutual vows. Why is this ritual dramatically important?
options: It satisfies a religious obligation before Othello can take revenge
It displaces Othello's marriage vow with a vow to Iago — tragically inverting intimacy
It seals a legal document admissible in Venetian courts
It represents Iago's genuine conversion to Othello's cause

trials.prompt: At the end of the scene, what role does Othello promise to Iago?
options: His second-in-command on the battlefield
His lieutenant, taking Cassio's former position
His ambassador to Venice
His personal bodyguard and translator
